response.original_title: What Remains at the Ends of the Earth?
response.overview: In the coastal wetlands of the US state of Louisiana, the fossil fuel industry reigns as heir to the extractive logics and landscapes of settler colonialism and slavery. Oil and gas corporations have dredged 10,000 miles of canals to drill and access 90,000 wells, which connect through 50,000 miles of pipeline to over 200 plants in the ‘Petrochemical Corridor’. This invasive infrastructure severs the integrity of Louisiana’s more-than-human ecologies. Canals kill the vegetation that holds sediment together as land; since the 1930s, 2,000 square miles of wetlands have disintegrated into the sea.
response.title: What Remains at the Ends of the Earth?

release_dates.note: 12th Berlin Biennale, Akademie der Künste, Hanseatenweg, 11.6.–18.9.2022
